title: Python Is So Slow. Can Julia Solve the Two-Language Problem?
gist: Fourteen years after its creation, Julia has not dethroned Python despite being 10x-1000x faster on some benchmarks, because Python's ecosystem/tooling moat and lack of Big Tech corporate patronage kept it niche; the author argues Julia's fate shows the scientific-computing 'two-language problem' (slow, friendly Python vs. fast, unfriendly C++/Rust) is structural and unlikely to be solved by any single language, including AI coding agents.

themes.theme: The two-language problem in scientific computing

insights.claim: Python dominates scientific and AI computing but is fundamentally slow, forcing researchers to prototype in Python and then rewrite performance-critical code in faster languages like C++ or Rust — a pattern the author calls 'the two-language problem.'
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: The persistent Python-to-C++/Rust rewrite cycle is a real engineering cost center in AI/scientific-computing shops, sustaining demand for performance-engineering talent and tools (e.g., Rust/C++ bindings, JIT compilers) regardless of AI coding-agent adoption.

insights.claim: The author argues this speed-vs-friendliness trade-off cannot be solved by deploying more AI coding agents, because no matter how much a slow language is optimized, a genuinely faster language will still outperform it.
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: A direct pushback on the narrative that AI coding agents will make language performance differences irrelevant — implies continued structural demand for systems languages and compiler/runtime innovation even in an AI-agent-heavy coding future.
insights.quote: This limitation can't be solved by spinning up a platoon of AI coding agents, because no matter how much you optimize a slow language, a faster one will outperform it.

insights.claim: The two-language trade-off recurs across software domains beyond scientific computing: game engines are written in C++ but scripted in Lua, server backends use Python/Ruby/JavaScript for ease but Go/Rust for performance-critical work, and attempts to use Go or Rust for frontend development have failed.
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: Generalizes the friendliness-vs-performance trade-off as a durable, cross-domain pattern in software engineering — a reason to expect continued fragmented tooling markets rather than one language consolidating developer spend.
insights.locator: While the problem is commonly referenced in scientific computing

insights.claim: Julia, created in 2012 by four computer scientists explicitly to solve the two-language problem, delivers dramatic real-world speedups — Julia-Con talks describe 60X speedups from rewriting MATLAB code, and by some benchmarks Julia runs 10X to 1,000X faster than Python — yet it did not replace Python and does not appear on Stack Overflow's most-popular-languages survey.
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: A large, well-funded, technically superior alternative failing to unseat an incumbent language demonstrates that raw performance advantage alone is insufficient to shift developer-ecosystem lock-in — a transferable lesson for evaluating any 'better but smaller' platform/tooling bet.

insights.claim: The author identifies two structural reasons Julia failed to unseat Python: Python's ecosystem and tooling are too robust to dislodge, and Julia has never received corporate patronage from a major tech company (unlike Objective-C, backed by Apple for iOS, or Kotlin, backed by Google for Android).
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: A concrete causal framework — ecosystem depth plus corporate sponsorship — for predicting which niche programming languages can break into mainstream adoption; useful lens for evaluating future language/tooling bets (e.g., Mojo, Zig) for corporate backing as an adoption catalyst.
insights.locator: First, just as a human language depends on the corpus

insights.claim: The author's own conclusion is that nothing 'went wrong' with Julia — it is a niche language that is plenty successful for its purpose, used for major work at institutions like ASML, CERN, and NASA, and in fields like drug discovery and advanced machine learning, and will likely remain 'small but beloved' rather than mainstream.
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: Reframes Julia not as a failed Python-killer but as a durable, defensible niche tool for high-value scientific/industrial users (chipmaking, particle physics, aerospace, drug discovery) — a specific target market for Julia-adjacent tooling and consulting businesses.
insights.locator: But third, and this is my answer: Nothing went wrong.

insights.claim: The author invokes Kenneth Iverson's 1979 Turing Award lecture 'Notation as a Tool of Thought' and APL (which turned 60 years old in 2026) as a historical precedent: APL proved that fusing 'thinking' notation and 'programming' notation into one language was possible, even though APL itself became more influential than widely adopted.
insights.speaker: Sheon Han
insights.so_what: Provides a 47-year-old case study (APL) showing that a technically elegant unifying language can shape an entire field's thinking (notation, tooling design) even without becoming the dominant production language — the same fate the article suggests await Julia.
insights.quote: By relieving the brain of all unnecessary work, a good notation sets it free to concentrate on more advanced problems.
insights.locator: As the mathematician Alfred North Whitehead once put it
